mnemonics.title: Risk Response Strategies
mnemonics.content: BATS - Beyond A Threat, Simply accept (Avoid, Transference, Acceptance, Mitigation)

mnemonics.title: Risk Identification Techniques
mnemonics.content: DBC - Delphi, Brainstorming, Checklist (three main identification techniques)

mnemonics.title: Risk Analysis Types
mnemonics.content: QQ - Quantitative uses Numbers, Qualitative uses Quality (subjective judgment)

acronyms.usage: Used to rank risks: RPN = Probability × Impact. Higher value = higher priority.
Average financial impact: EMV = Probability × Impact (monetary). Used in decision trees.
Measures cost efficiency: CPI = Earned Value / Actual Cost. Below 1 indicates over budget.
Measures schedule efficiency: SPI = Earned Value / Planned Value. Below 1 indicates behind schedule.
Includes prevention, appraisal, and failure costs. Used in risk-related quality decisions.
Framework for risk identification considering both positive and negative factors.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a 2D grid where X-axis is Probability (1-5) and Y-axis is Impact (1-5). The diagonal line from bottom-left to top-right divides low (below line) from high (above line) priority risks. Draw your specific risk as a dot - its distance from origin shows priority.
A 3×3 grid: Low/Medium/High probability vs Low/Medium/High impact. Color code: Green (low-low), Yellow (others), Red (high-high). Helps visually categorize and prioritize risks at a glance.

Draw a tree where branches show different risk outcomes. At each branch point, calculate EMV by multiplying probability (as decimal) with the monetary value at the end. Sum all branch EMVs to get expected value.

analogies.analogy: Like a fire alert system - a small fire (low impact) in an empty building (low probability) is low priority (low RPN), but a large fire (high impact) in an occupied building (high probability) demands immediate action (high RPN).
Like buying car insurance - you transfer the financial risk of accidents to the insurance company by paying a premium (mitigation cost). The risk still exists but its impact on you is reduced.
Like a doctor doing a medical checkup - before treatment (risk response), the doctor must first identify (diagnose) what could go wrong with the patient's health (project).
Qualitative is like estimating room temperature by feeling it (warm/cool). Quantitative is like using a thermometer to get exact temperature (25°C). Both useful but at different precision levels.
